facts.text: Jean-Jacques Rousseau was born at Geneva in 1712.
Rousseau was the son of Isaac Rousseau and Susannah Bernard.
Rousseau's father was a watchmaker.
Rousseau's mother was the daughter of a minister named Bernard.
Rousseau's parents knew each other from childhood, walking together on the banks of the Treille at eight or nine years old.
Rousseau's uncle Gabriel Bernard married one of Rousseau's father's sisters.
Rousseau had one brother.
Rousseau's father traveled to Constantinople and was appointed watchmaker to the Seraglio.
Rousseau's uncle Bernard was an engineer who served under Prince Eugene.
Rousseau's uncle distinguished himself at the siege and battle of Belgrade.
Rousseau's mother attracted many admirers during her husband's absence.
Monsieur de la Closure, Resident of France, was assiduous in his attentions to Rousseau's mother.
Rousseau intended his autobiography to show himself 'Intus et in cute' to posterity.
Rousseau's faults were generally due to defective education, acute sensitiveness, suspicion, irresolution, and an overstrained sense of honour and independence.
Rousseau was afflicted during much of his life with an incurable disease.
Rousseau renounced his duty as a father to his offspring.
Rousseau loved Madame de Warens deeply and sincerely.
Rousseau had a profound sense of justice and a real desire for the improvement and advancement of the race.
Rousseau was beloved even by persons whom he tried to repel.
Rousseau wrote 'La Nouvelle Heloise'.
Rousseau wrote 'Reveries d'un Promeneur Solitaire'.
Rousseau wrote 'Emile'.
Rousseau wrote 'Contrat Social'.
Rousseau wrote 'Lettres Ecrites de la Montagne'.
Rousseau's 'La Nouvelle Heloise' had a profound effect on the public mind.
Rousseau depicted nature as she really is in 'La Nouvelle Heloise'.
Rousseau poured a tide of passion into 'La Nouvelle Heloise'.
Voltaire had slight sympathy with Rousseau's notions.
Voltaire used sarcasm and ridicule against Rousseau.
Rousseau traveled to Strasbourg after quitting the Ile de Saint-Pierre.
Rousseau arrived in Paris on December 16, 1765.
The Prince de Conti provided Rousseau with lodging in the Hotel Saint-Simon.
Horace Walpole wrote an ironical letter purporting to come from Frederick the Great.
Rousseau ascribed the forged letter to Voltaire.
David Hume persuaded Rousseau to go to England in early 1766.
Edmund Burke held that inordinate vanity was the leading trait in Rousseau's character.
Mr. Davenport offered Rousseau a home at Wootton in Staffordshire.
Rousseau accepted the offer at Wootton on condition that he pay a rent of 30 pounds a year.
George III accorded Rousseau a pension of 100 pounds.
Rousseau declined to draw the pension after the first annual payment.
Rousseau lived at Wootton with Therese.
Rousseau devoted his time to herborising and writing the first six books of his Confessions at Wootton.
Rousseau became convinced that enemies were bent upon his capture or death.
Rousseau wrote a violent letter to Hume in June 1766, calling him 'one of the worst of men'.
Rousseau fled Wootton in spring 1767.
Rousseau fled first to Spalding in Lincolnshire.
Rousseau subsequently fled to Calais, where he landed in May.
Rousseau was titled 'Voyageur Perpetuel' due to his restless and wandering disposition.
Rousseau wrote the second part of the Confessions while at Trye in Gisors in 1767-8.
Rousseau assumed the surname Renou.
Rousseau declared before two witnesses that Therese was his wife.
Rousseau took up abode in Paris in 1770.
Rousseau lived in Paris continuously for seven years.
Rousseau lived in a street in Paris that now bears his name.
Rousseau gained a living by copying music in Paris.
Bernardin de Saint-Pierre became acquainted with Rousseau in 1772.
Monsieur de Girardin offered Rousseau an asylum at Ermemonville in spring 1778.
Rousseau died on July 3, 1778, stricken by apoplexy.
A rumor circulated that Rousseau had committed suicide.
Rousseau's remains were first interred in the Ile des Peupliers.
Rousseau's remains were removed to the Pantheon after the Revolution.
The Government of Geneva erected a statue of Rousseau modelled by Pradier on an island in the Rhone.
Rousseau claimed to know his heart and to have studied mankind.
Rousseau claimed he was not made like anyone he had been acquainted with.
Rousseau claimed originality in his nature.
Rousseau related both laudable and wicked actions in his Confessions.
Rousseau concealed no crimes and added no virtues in his Confessions.
Rousseau sometimes introduced superfluous ornament to occupy voids occasioned by defect of memory.
Rousseau never asserted a conscious falsehood as truth in his Confessions.
Jean-Jacques Rousseau's uncle Gabriel Bernard fell in love with one of Rousseau's father's sisters.
Gabriel Bernard was an engineer.
Gabriel Bernard served in the empire and Hungary under Prince Eugene.
Gabriel Bernard distinguished himself at the siege and battle of Belgrade.
Rousseau's father set off for Constantinople after the birth of Rousseau's only brother.
Rousseau's father was appointed watchmaker to the Seraglio.
Rousseau's mother was beautiful, witty, and accomplished.
Rousseau's mother was taught drawing, singing, and to play on the theorbo.
Rousseau's mother had learning and wrote agreeable verses.
Rousseau's mother tenderly loved Rousseau's father.
Rousseau's mother died from complications of Rousseau's birth.
Rousseau was born ten months after his father's return to Geneva.
Rousseau was born in a very weakly and infirm state.
Rousseau's birth cost his mother her life.
Rousseau's father was inconsolable after his wife's death.
Rousseau's father could never forget that Rousseau had been the innocent cause of his misfortune.
Rousseau's father never embraced Rousseau without sighs and convulsive pressure of his arms.
Rousseau's father expired forty years after his wife's loss in the arms of his second wife.
Rousseau's father's first wife's name still vibrated on his lips at his death.
Rousseau inherited a feeling heart from his parents.
Rousseau came into the world with few signs of life.
Rousseau had a disorder from birth that gathered strength with years.
Rousseau's father's sister took tender care of Rousseau in infancy.
Rousseau's father's sister was living at an advanced age nursing a husband younger than herself.
Rousseau's father's sister's husband was worn out with excessive drinking.
Rousseau's nurse was named Jaqueline.
Rousseau has no knowledge of what passed prior to his fifth or sixth year.
Rousseau does not recollect learning to read.
Rousseau dates his uninterrupted knowledge of himself from when he first read considerably.
Every night after supper, Rousseau and his father read from a small collection of romances that had been his mother's.
Rousseau and his father would read whole nights together and could not bear to give over until the conclusion of a volume.
Rousseau acquired an extreme facility in reading and comprehending through reading romances nightly with his father.
Rousseau acquired a too intimate acquaintance with the passions for his age through reading romances.
Rousseau's romance reading concluded with the summer of 1719.
Rousseau's mother's library was exhausted by the winter following summer 1719.
Rousseau's grandfather was a minister.
Rousseau's grandfather had learning and taste and good sense.
Rousseau read history of the Church and Empire by Le Sueur.
Rousseau read Bossuett's Discourses on Universal History.
Rousseau read Plutarch's Lives.
Rousseau read the history of Venice by Nani.
Rousseau read Ovid's Metamorphoses.
Rousseau read La Bruyere.
Rousseau read Fontenelle's World and Dialogues of the Dead.
Rousseau read volumes of Moliere.
Plutarch became Rousseau's greatest favorite.
Rousseau preferred Agesilaus, Brutus, and Aristides to Orondates, Artemenes, and Juba.
Rousseau developed a republican spirit and love of liberty from reading Plutarch.
Rousseau developed a haughty and invincible turn of mind that rendered him impatient of restraint or servitude.
Rousseau was born the citizen of a republic.
Rousseau's father's ruling passion was a love of his country.
Rousseau could fancy himself a Greek or Roman while reading.
Rousseau was transported by recitals of extraordinary instances of fortitude or intrepidity.
Rousseau once held his hand over a hot chafing-dish while relating the fortitude of Scoevola.
Rousseau's brother was seven years older than himself.
Rousseau's brother was brought up to his father's profession.
Rousseau's brother was too much neglected in his education.
Rousseau's brother acquired the habits of a libertine before arriving at an age to be really one.
Rousseau's father tried placing his brother with a master, but the brother persisted in ill conduct.
Rousseau saw his brother so seldom that they were hardly acquainted.
Rousseau loved his brother tenderly.
Rousseau once threw himself between his father and brother to protect his brother from being beaten.
Rousseau's brother's conduct became so bad that he suddenly disappeared.
Rousseau's brother went to Germany.
Rousseau's brother never wrote to the family.
Rousseau became an only son after his brother disappeared.
Rousseau was treated with more attention and tenderness than a king's children in his infancy.
Rousseau was the darling of the family.
Rousseau was treated as a beloved child but never a spoiled child.
Rousseau was never permitted to play in the street with other children while under paternal inspection.
Rousseau had the faults common to his age: he was talkative, a glutton, and sometimes a liar.
Rousseau made no scruple of stealing sweetmeats, fruits, or eatables.
Rousseau never took delight in mischievous waste, in accusing others, or tormenting harmless animals.
Rousseau once made water in Madame Clot's kettle while she was at church.
Rousseau had before his eyes only examples of mildness in his childhood.
Rousseau was surrounded by some of the best people in the world in his childhood.
Rousseau found so little to excite his desires that he was hardly sensible of possessing any.
Rousseau was an absolute stranger to caprice until after he experienced the authority of a master.
Rousseau spent hours with his aunt seeing her embroider or hearing her sing.
Rousseau's aunt's tenderness and unaffected gayety left indelible impressions on his mind.
Rousseau can describe his aunt's clothes and head-dress in detail.
Rousseau remembers the two curls of fine black hair which hung on his aunt's temples.
Rousseau's taste or passion for music did not show itself until a considerable time after his childhood.
Rousseau is indebted to his aunt for his passion for music.
Rousseau's aunt knew a great number of songs which she sang with great sweetness and melody.
Rousseau's aunt's serenity and cheerfulness banished melancholy and made all around her happy.
Several of Rousseau's aunt's songs have remained in his memory since infancy.
Some of Rousseau's aunt's songs return to Rousseau's mind as he grows old with inexpressible charm.
Rousseau sometimes weeps like a child when recalling songs from his infancy.
Rousseau perfectly recollects the tune of a particular song from his infancy but cannot recall all the words.
Rousseau's heart feels an invincible charm on recollection of the song fragment from his infancy.
Rousseau's voice is interrupted by tenderness and his eyes suffused with tears when recalling the song.
Rousseau has formed the resolution of writing to Paris for the remainder of the song's words.
Rousseau is almost certain the pleasure in recollection would be diminished if anyone but his aunt Susan had sung the song.
Rousseau's heart is at once haughty and tender.
Rousseau's character is effeminate yet invincible.
Rousseau fluctuates between weakness and courage, luxury and virtue.
Rousseau has set himself in contradiction to himself throughout his life.
Rousseau's father had a quarrel with M. G----, who had a captain's commission in France.
M. G---- was related to several of the Council.
M. G---- was an insolent, ungenerous man.
M. G---- accused Rousseau's father of having drawn his sword on him in the city.
Rousseau's father was about to be conducted to prison because of M. G----'s accusation.
Rousseau's father insisted that the accuser should be confined at the same time.
Rousseau's father preferred voluntary banishment for the remainder of his life to giving up a point by which he must sacrifice his honor and liberty.
Rousseau remained under the tuition of his uncle Bernard after his father's banishment.
Rousseau's uncle Bernard was employed in the fortifications of Geneva.
Rousseau's uncle Bernard had lost his eldest daughter.
Rousseau's uncle Bernard had a son about Rousseau's own age.
Rousseau and his cousin were sent together to Bossey to board with the Minister Lambercier.
Rousseau was to learn Latin at Bossey.
Two years spent at Bossey softened Rousseau's Roman fierceness.
Two years at Bossey reduced Rousseau to a state of childhood.
At Geneva, Rousseau loved reading, which was his principal amusement.
At Bossey, Rousseau was fond of play as a relaxation.
The country at Bossey was new and charming in Rousseau's idea.
Rousseau conceived a passion for rural life at Bossey which time has not been able to extinguish.
Rousseau has never ceased to regret the pure and tranquil pleasures he enjoyed at Bossey in his childhood.
M. Lambercier was a worthy, sensible man.
M. Lambercier never made Rousseau's acquisitions burdensome or tasks tedious.
Rousseau has an extreme aversion to restraint.
Rousseau's recollection of his studies at Bossey is never attended with disgust.
Rousseau's improvement at Bossey was trivial but obtained with ease and has never escaped memory.
The simplicity of rural life at Bossey was of infinite advantage in opening Rousseau's heart to the reception of true friendship.
Rousseau's sentiments on friendship before Bossey were extremely elevated but altogether imaginary.
Rousseau became tenderly united to his cousin Bernard at Bossey.
Rousseau's affection for his cousin Bernard was more ardent than that he had felt for his brother.
Time has never been able to efface Rousseau's affection for his cousin Bernard.
Rousseau's cousin Bernard was a tall, lank, weakly boy.
Rousseau's cousin Bernard had a mind as mild as his body was feeble.
Jean-Jacques Rousseau regretted leaving the pure and tranquil pleasures he enjoyed at Bossey in his childhood.
M. Lambercier was a worthy, sensible man who did not make instruction burdensome or tasks tedious.
Despite Rousseau's extreme aversion to restraint, his recollection of his studies was never attended with disgust.
The rural life at Bossey was advantageous in opening Rousseau's heart to true friendship.
Rousseau's sentiments about friendship before Bossey were extremely elevated but altogether imaginary.
Rousseau became tenderly united to his cousin Bernard through living in a peaceful manner.
Bernard was a tall, lank, weakly boy with a mild mind and feeble body.
Rousseau and Bernard were inseparable during the five years they were at Bossey and Geneva.
Rousseau and Bernard often fought but never had quarrels lasting more than a quarter of an hour.
Rousseau and Bernard never made complaints of each other.
Rousseau possessed less natural vanity than most individuals.
Rousseau's most ardent wish was to be loved by everyone who knew him.
Rousseau was naturally mild.
During two years at Bossey, Rousseau was neither the victim nor witness of any violent emotions.
Rousseau found it delightful to see everyone content.
Rousseau was more afflicted by Miss Lambercier's disapprobation than by punishment.
Miss Lambercier sometimes inflicted on Rousseau and Bernard the punishment of infants.
Rousseau found the reality of Miss Lambercier's punishment much less terrible than the idea of it.
Rousseau's affection for Miss Lambercier increased after she inflicted punishment on him.
A degree of sensuality mingled with the smart and shame of Rousseau's punishment.
Rousseau sought by fresh offences a return of the same chastisement from Miss Lambercier.
Rousseau would have reacted differently to the same discipline from Miss Lambercier's brother.
Benevolence aided by passions has maintained an empire over Rousseau's heart.
Miss Lambercier renounced corporal punishment after the second time, declaring it too fatiguing.
Rousseau and Bernard slept in Miss Lambercier's chamber and during winter in her bed.
After the second punishment, another room was prepared for Rousseau and Bernard.
Rousseau received childish discipline at eight years old from Miss Lambercier, a woman of thirty.
The incident of corporal punishment influenced Rousseau's propensities, desires, and passions for the rest of his life.
The incident that inflamed Rousseau's senses gave his desires an extraordinary turn.
Rousseau sought no further gratification beyond what he had already experienced with Miss Lambercier.
Rousseau preserved his purity beyond the age when coldest constitutions lose their insensibility.
Rousseau gazed on every handsome woman with delight and transformed them into Miss Lamberciers in his imagination.
Rousseau's education was perfectly chaste.
Rousseau's three aunts were of exemplary prudence and maintained modest reserve.
Rousseau's father loved pleasure but his gallantry was of the last rather than the present century.
A good maid-servant was discharged at M. Lambercier's for making an indelicate expression before the children.
Rousseau had no precise idea of the ultimate effect of the passions but found the conception extremely disgusting.
Rousseau entertained a particular aversion for courtesans.
Rousseau could not look on a rake without disdain mingled with terror.
Rousseau's prejudices of education were strengthened by the effect of his first moments of sensuality.
Rousseau passed the age of puberty without knowing or wishing for any gratification of passions beyond what Miss Lambercier had given him an idea of.
Rousseau's childish taste associated with other passions when he became a man.
Rousseau's natural timidity prevented him from being very enterprising with women.
Rousseau passed his days languishing in silence for women he most admired without daring to disclose his wishes.
Rousseau found the most exquisite enjoyment in falling at the feet of an imperious mistress and obeying her mandates.
Rousseau's mode of making love was not attended with rapid progress or danger to the virtue of its object.
Rousseau has had few favors to boast of but has not been excluded from imaginary enjoyment.
Rousseau's senses and timid romantic mind have preserved his moral chasteness and uncorrupted feelings.
Rousseau found it more difficult to divulge what is ridiculous than what is really criminal.
During his whole life, Rousseau could never acquire sufficient resolution to declare his folly to women even in unbounded familiarity.
One day while studying, Rousseau was accused of breaking the teeth of one of Miss Lambercier's combs.
Rousseau denied knowledge of breaking the comb and obstinately persisted in the denial.
This was the first time Rousseau had been detected in a confirmed falsehood.
Rousseau's uncle Bernard was written to and arrived to administer punishment.
Rousseau's cousin Bernard was charged with a crime and conducted to the same execution as Rousseau.
The punishment was inflicted with great severity.
Rousseau was freed from the dominion of his depraved desires for a long time after the severe punishment.
Rousseau's obstinacy brought on several repetitions of punishment and reduced him to a deplorable situation.
Rousseau was immovable and resolutely determined to suffer death rather than submit to admitting guilt.
Fifty years after the comb incident, Rousseau avered in the face of Heaven that he was absolutely innocent.
Rousseau never broke, touched, or came near the fire where the comb was.
Rousseau's leading character traits were docility and timidity but also haughtiness, ardor, and invincibility in passions.
Rousseau's reason was not sufficiently established to enable him to judge how much appearances condemned him.
The smart Rousseau suffered from punishment was inconsiderable compared to what he felt from indignation, rage, and despair.
Rousseau's cousin became furious by Rousseau's example of being punished for a crime he did not commit.
Rousseau and his cousin embraced each other with convulsive transport in the same bed after punishment.
Rousseau and his cousin repeated 'Carnifex!' a hundred times to express their indignation.
The first instance of violence and oppression is deeply engraved on Rousseau's soul.
Every relative idea to the comb incident renews Rousseau's emotion.
Rousseau's sentiment of indignation has become detached from personal motives and applies to any act of injustice.
Rousseau's heart is inflamed at the sight or relation of any act of injustice as if he was the immediate sufferer.
Rousseau could instantly set off to stab tyrants or knavish priests upon reading of their misdeeds.
Rousseau has frequently fatigued himself by running after and stoning animals he saw tormenting other animals.
Rousseau's natural inclination to oppose injustice was strengthened by the lively impression of his first injustice.
The comb incident terminated Rousseau's infantine serenity.
From the comb incident onward, Rousseau ceased to enjoy pure unadulterated happiness.
Rousseau and his cousin remained at Bossey some months after the comb incident.
After the comb incident, Rousseau and his cousin were like Adam and Eve after losing their innocence.
Affection, respect, intimacy, and confidence no longer attached the pupils to their guides after the comb incident.
Rousseau and his cousin no longer beheld their guides as divinities who could read the secrets of their hearts.
Rousseau and his cousin learned to dissemble, rebel, and lie after the comb incident.
All the vices common to their years began to corrupt Rousseau and his cousin's happy innocence.
The country lost its sweet and simple charms and appeared a gloomy desert to Rousseau after the comb incident.
Rousseau and his cousin cultivated their little gardens no more after the comb incident.
Rousseau and his cousin were disgusted with their situation at Bossey after the comb incident.
Rousseau's uncle wrote for their return from Bossey.
Rousseau and his cousin left Mr. and Miss Lambercier without feeling any regret at the separation.
After losing their innocence, the pupils no longer felt affection, respect, intimacy, and confidence toward their guides.
The pupils learned to dissemble, rebel, and lie after the loss of their innocence.
The country lost its sweet and simple charms and appeared as a gloomy desert to the pupils.
The pupils neglected their little gardens and flowers.
Nearly thirty years passed after Rousseau left Bossey before he recalled the place with satisfaction.
In old age, Rousseau's memories of Bossey revived with increasing force and charm.
Rousseau can recall minute details of his time at Bossey, including specific furniture and objects.
Rousseau felt a necessity to recount the details of his time at Bossey.
Rousseau preferred the anecdote of the walnut tree because he was an actor in it rather than a spectator.
Mr. Lambercier planted a walnut tree on the terrace with great solemnity.
Rousseau and his cousin decided to plant a willow tree on the terrace to rival the walnut tree.
Rousseau and his cousin created an underground furrow to divert water from the walnut tree to their willow.
Rousseau and his cousin's water diversion system initially succeeded in bringing water to their willow.
Mr. Lambercier discovered the underground channel and destroyed it with a pickaxe while exclaiming 'an aqueduct!'
Mr. Lambercier did not reproach Rousseau and his cousin for the aqueduct incident.
Mr. Lambercier laughed about the aqueduct incident with his sister.
Rousseau and his cousin planted another tree after the walnut tree catastrophe.
The walnut tree anecdote was the first visible effect of Rousseau's vanity.
Rousseau considered constructing the aqueduct a supreme degree of glory.
Rousseau had a conception of glory at age ten that exceeded Caesar's at age thirty.
Rousseau desired to visit Bossey in 1754 to see the walnut tree but was prevented by company.
Rousseau would water the walnut tree with his tears if he found it still existing.
Rousseau's uncle's son was devoted to genius and was taught drawing and Euclid.
Rousseau was principally fond of drawing during his time with his uncle.
Rousseau's friends were irresolute about whether to make him a watchmaker, lawyer, or minister.
Rousseau preferred to be a minister because he thought preaching would be charming.
Rousseau's mother's small income was to be divided between him and his brother.
The income was too inconsiderate to defray the expense of pursuing ministerial studies.
Rousseau remained with his uncle for several years after returning to Geneva.
Rousseau's uncle was a man of pleasure who did not abridge his amusements for family instruction.
Rousseau's aunt was a devotee who loved singing psalms.
Rousseau and his cousin were left entirely to themselves but never abused their liberty.
Rousseau and his cousin were inseparable and felt no inclination to frequent disorderly lads.
Rousseau and his cousin made cages, pipes, kites, drums, houses, ships, and bows.
Rousseau and his cousin spoiled their grandfather's tools by attempting to make watches.
Rousseau and his cousin's favorite amusement was wasting paper in drawing, washing, and coloring.
Rousseau and his cousin saw an exhibition of puppets by an Italian mountebank called Gamber-Corta.
Rousseau and his cousin made puppets of their own and invented comedies.
Rousseau and his cousin mimicked the voice of Punch in their puppet performances.
Rousseau's aunt and uncle Bernard watched and listened to their puppet comedies.
After Rousseau's uncle read an elaborate discourse, Rousseau and his cousin gave up comedies and began composing sermons.
Rousseau and his cousin were absolute masters of their time and found no inclination to abuse it.
Rousseau and his cousin were inseparable and constantly neglected occasions to seek other companions.
Rousseau's cousin was very tall while Rousseau was extremely short.
Rousseau and his cousin were nicknamed 'Barna Bredanna' by other children.
Rousseau was instantly for fighting when mocked, while his cousin bore indignity with patience.
Rousseau engaged in fights with children who mocked him and his cousin.
Rousseau's cousin was weak and could not effectively assist him in fights.
Rousseau became a redresser of grievances in his youth.
Rousseau frequently visited his father at Nion in the Vaudois country.
Rousseau's father was universally respected in Nion.
Madame de Vulson loaded Rousseau with caresses during his visits to Nion.
Madame de Vulson's daughter made Rousseau her gallant when he was eleven years old.
Rousseau saw no inequality between himself and Miss Vulson despite the age difference.
Rousseau's passion for Miss Vulson transported him almost to madness.
Rousseau experienced two kinds of love that were equally real but had scarce any affinity.
Rousseau's whole life was divided between different affections.
Rousseau frequently felt the power of multiple affections at the same instant.
Rousseau publicly and tyrannically claimed Miss Vulson and could not suffer other men to approach her.
Rousseau had secret assignations with Miss Goton, who acted as a schoolmistress with him.
Rousseau felt the whole charm of mystery in his meetings with Miss Goton.
Rousseau repaid Miss Vulson in kind by concealing his amours with Miss Goton.
Rousseau's secret with Miss Goton was discovered and he lost his young schoolmistress.
Miss Goton was not handsome but had a certain something in her figure that was not easily forgotten.
Miss Goton had eyes that did not correspond with her age, height, or manner.
Miss Goton had a lofty imposing air that agreed with the character she assumed.
Miss Goton took great liberties with Rousseau but would not permit any to be taken with her in return.
Miss Goton treated Rousseau precisely like a child.
Rousseau was absolutely in the power of both Miss Vulson and Miss Goton.
When in the presence of either mistress, Rousseau never thought of the other.
Rousseau could have passed his whole life with Miss Vulson without forming a wish to quit her.
Rousseau's satisfaction with Miss Vulson was attended with pleasing serenity.
Rousseau was particularly charmed with Miss Vulson in numerous companies.
Miss Vulson's sprightly wit and arch glance strengthened Rousseau's attachment.
Rousseau triumphed in the preference Miss Vulson seemed to bestow on him over more powerful rivals.
Rousseau felt the whole force of love when surrounded by observers of his relationship with Miss Vulson.
Rousseau would have been constrained and unhappy in a tete-a-tete with Miss Vulson.
Rousseau was in the power of two mistresses, Miss Vulson and Miss Goton.
Rousseau could have passed his whole life with Miss Vulson without wishing to leave her.
Rousseau experienced jealousy toward Miss Vulson despite loving her with a brother's affection only.
Rousseau felt bewildered and absorbed in ecstasy at the sight of Miss Goton.
Rousseau was more complaisant with Miss Vulson and more submissive with Miss Goton.
Rousseau would have obeyed Miss Goton if she commanded him to throw himself into flames.
Rousseau's connection with Miss Vulson lasted longer than his connection with Miss Goton.
Rousseau and Miss Vulson separated with tears.
Rousseau and Miss Vulson agreed to correspond with each other during their separation.
Miss Vulson came to see Rousseau at Geneva.
Rousseau was intoxicated with delight during the two days Miss Vulson remained in Geneva.
Rousseau learned of Miss Vulson's marriage at the same time she sent him sweetmeats and gloves.
Rousseau swore never to see Miss Vulson again after learning of her marriage.
Rousseau encountered Miss Vulson again twenty years later on a lake near Geneva.
Rousseau ordered the waterman to turn away rather than renew contact with Miss Vulson after twenty years.
Rousseau was sent to Mr. Masseron, the City Register, to learn the occupation of a scraper.
Rousseau found the prospect of the scraper occupation displeasing.
Rousseau felt horror at entering Mr. Masseron's office.
Mr. Masseron treated Rousseau with disdain and called him a fool and blockhead.
Rousseau was turned out of the registry and pronounced a fool by Mr. Masseron's clerks.
Rousseau was bound apprentice to an engraver named M. Ducommon.
M. Ducommon was a young man of violent and boorish character.
M. Ducommon tarnished Rousseau's amiable qualities and reduced him to a state of servitude.
Rousseau forgot his Latin, history, and antiquities during his apprenticeship.
Rousseau avoided visiting Mr. and Miss Lambercier after his apprenticeship began.
Rousseau had a lively taste for drawing.
Rousseau did not find the exercise of the graver displeasing.
Rousseau hoped to attain perfection as a watchcase engraver.
Rousseau engraved medals with his companions as insignia for an invented order of chivalry.
Rousseau's master caught him engraving medals and gave him a severe beating.
Rousseau's master accused him of attempting to make counterfeit money.
Rousseau had no conception of false money and little knowledge of true money.
Rousseau's master's tyranny drove him to vices he naturally despised: falsehood, idleness, and theft.
Rousseau had enjoyed reasonable liberty at his father's house.
Rousseau was free at Mr. Lambercier's house.
Rousseau was discreet at his uncle's house.
Rousseau became fearful with his master.
Rousseau was accustomed to live on terms of perfect equality before his apprenticeship.
Rousseau was scarce allowed to speak at his master's house.
Rousseau was forced to quit the table without tasting what he longed for.
Rousseau learned to covet, dissemble, lie, and steal during his apprenticeship.
Rousseau never felt the propensity to steal before his apprenticeship.
Rousseau has never been able to entirely divest himself of the propensity to steal since his apprenticeship.
Rousseau did not steal eatables for more than a year despite continual wants and temptations.
Rousseau's first theft was occasioned by complaisance.
Rousseau's master had a journeyman named Verrat.
Verrat's mother lived in the neighborhood and had a garden that produced excellent asparagus.
Verrat proposed to Rousseau that they rob asparagus from his mother's garden.
Rousseau could never resist the attacks of flattery.
Rousseau gathered asparagus from Verrat's mother's garden every morning.
Rousseau took the stolen asparagus to the Holard where old women bought it at reduced prices.
Rousseau never partook of the breakfast made from the stolen asparagus.
Rousseau executed his roguery with the greatest fidelity, seeking only to please his employer.
Rousseau later began to rob Verrat's harvest in addition to his mother's garden.
Rousseau discovered through his expeditions that it was not as terrible to thieve as he had imagined.
Rousseau was not absolutely ill-fed at his master's house.
Rousseau's temperance was only painful to him by comparing it with the luxury his master enjoyed.
Rousseau attempted to procure apples from his master's pantry.
Rousseau used spits to try to reach apples in the pantry.
Rousseau lengthened one spit with a smaller one used for game.
Rousseau successfully brought up an apple but found it too large to pass through the lattice.
Rousseau divided the apple with a knife but both pieces fell into the pantry.
Rousseau attempted to procure the apples again the next day.
Rousseau's master discovered him attempting to steal the apples and exclaimed "Bravo!".
Rousseau experienced horror at being discovered by his master.
Rousseau became callous from continual ill treatment.
Rousseau viewed punishment as a composition for his crimes that authorized him to continue them.
Rousseau judged he had a right to all the vices of a slave because he was beaten like one.
Rousseau was convinced that to rob and be punished were inseparable.
Rousseau applied himself to thieving with great tranquility after accepting punishment as inevitable.
Rousseau returned to his work without thinking about witnesses to his misdeed.
Rousseau attempted to use spits as weapons against prey in the pantry.
Rousseau's master discovered him during his second attempt and exclaimed 'Bravo!'
